sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 34, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Token at [30,30]-[32,32] blocked by purple wall. UP didn't move it (only HUD changed). Need to go LEFT back to platform, then find path around wall.
- Plan: Move the active right token UP over the purple wall, RIGHT past it, then DOWN into the right container.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 33 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 1.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

Now I can see the board clearly. The token (NNN/NWN/NNN) is at rows 30-32, cols 27-29 (0-indexed from the full grid, but in the cropped view it's at cols 7-9 of the cropped region starting at col 20, so cols 27-29 of the full grid).

- Row 30: `wwwwwwwwwwNNNppppppwwwwwc`
- Row 31: `wwwwwwwwwwNWNppppppwwwwww`

```
Row 30: wwwwwwwwwwNNNppppppwwwwwc

```
Position 0 in the output is col 20 in the full grid.
Position 10 is col 30.
So 'NNN' starts at position 10, which is col 30.
So the token is at cols 30-32, rows 30-32. But the segmentation says [30,27]-[32,29]. There's a 3-column discrepancy.
Hmm, maybe the segmentation boundary is using a different coordinate system? Or maybe I'm misreading it. Let me just trust the ascii for now.
OK so the token is at rows 30-32, cols 30-32. The purple wall is at cols 33-38 (6 columns of 'p'). The token is right next to the wall.
Now, looking at the board, I see that the token is on the bridge (white surface) at rows 30-32. The bridge connects the left platform to the purple wall. The left platform is at rows 21-32, cols 9-29 (approximately).
Wait, looking at the ascii more carefully:
- Row 21: `wwwwgggggggggppppppwwwwww`
- Row 22-29: same pattern
- Row 30: `wwwwwwwwwwNNNppppppwwwwwc`
- Row 31: `wwwwwwwwwwNWNppppppwwwwww`
- Row 32: `wwwwwwwwwwNNNppppppwwwwww`
- Row 33-41: same as rows 21-29
So the left platform (white 'w') is at cols 0-3 of the cropped region (cols 20-23 of the full grid), and the bridge is at cols 4-9 (cols 24-29). The token is at cols 10-12 (cols 30-32).
Wait, that doesn't match. Let me recount.
Row 21: `wwwwgggggggggppppppwwwwww`
- Cols 20-23: 'wwww' (4 chars) - left platform
- Cols 24-32: 'ggggggggggg' (11 chars) - gray background? No, this is between the platforms.
- Cols 33-38: 'pppppp' (6 chars) - purple wall
- Cols 39-46: 'wwwwww' (6 chars) - right platform
Hmm, but row 30 is different:
Row 30: `wwwwwwwwwwNNNppppppwwwwwc`
- Cols 20-29: 'wwwwwwwwww' (10 chars) - bridge (white)
- Cols 30-32: 'NNN' (3 chars) - token
- Cols 33-38: 'pppppp' (6 chars) - purple wall
- Cols 39-43: 'wwwww' (5 chars) - right platform
- Col 44: 'c' - container border?
So the bridge is at cols 20-29, rows 30-32. The token is at cols 30-32, rows 30-32. The purple wall is at cols 33-38, rows 21-41 (all rows).
Wait, but in rows 21-29, the left platform is at cols 20-23 ('wwww'), and cols 24-32 are 'g' (gray background). So the left platform is narrower at the top, and the bridge extends from the left platform at rows 30-32.
Actually, I think I need to look at the full board to understand the layout. But for now, let me focus on the token's position and the wall.
The token is at rows 30-32, cols 30-32. The wall is at cols 33-38. The token is right next to the wall.
Now, the key question is: can the token move RIGHT past the wall? From the ascii, the wall is at cols 33-38, rows 21-41. The token is at rows 30-32. If the token moves RIGHT, it would go to cols 33-35, which overlaps with the wall. So the wall blocks.
But wait, looking at row 21:
```
Row 21: wwwwgggggggggppppppwwwwww
```
The wall is at cols 33-38 ('pppppp'). Above the wall (rows < 21), there's no wall. So if the token can move to rows < 21, it might be able to go around the wall.
But the left platform is at rows 21-32 (based on the ascii). The top of the platform is at row 21. If the token moves UP from row 21, it goes to row 18, which is outside the platform. But there's no surface there, so the token can't move.
Hmm, but wait. Looking at the ascii, row 20 is:
```
Row 20: ggggggggggggggggggggggggg
```
All gray. So there's no surface above row 21. The token can't move above the platform.
OK so the token is stuck. It can't move RIGHT (wall), UP (no surface), or DOWN (no surface at rows > 32 on the bridge). The only option is LEFT.
But wait, I just realized something. Looking at the ascii again:
```
Row 30: wwwwwwwwwwNNNppppppwwwwwc
```
The token is at cols 30-32. But the bridge is at cols 20-29. So the token is NOT on the bridge; it's in the gap between the bridge and the wall.
Actually, looking more carefully, the bridge extends from cols 20-29, and the token is at cols 30-32. So the token moved RIGHT from the bridge into the gap. But the gap is only 3 columns wide (cols 30-32), and the token is 3 columns wide. So the token fills the gap.
Now, if I move the token LEFT, it goes back onto the bridge (cols 27-29). From there, I can move UP or DOWN on the bridge.
But the bridge is only at rows 30-32. If I move UP from the bridge, the token goes to rows 27-29. But rows 27-29 at cols 27-29 are 'g' (gray background), not a surface. So the token can't move UP from the bridge.
Wait, but earlier I moved the token UP from [30,24]-[32,26] to [27,24]-[29,26], and it worked. So the token CAN move UP from the platform/bridge.
Hmm, let me re-examine. The left platform is at rows 21-32, cols 20-23 (based on the ascii). The bridge is at rows 30-32, cols 24-29. So the platform and bridge form a continuous surface at rows 30-32, cols 20-29.
If the token is at rows 30-32, cols 24-26 (on the bridge), it can move UP to rows 27-29. But rows 27-29 at cols 24-26 are... let me check the ascii.
Row 27: `wwwwgggggggggppppppwwwwwc`
- Cols 20-23: 'wwww' (platform)
- Cols 24-32: 'ggggggggggg' (gray background)
So rows 27-29 at cols 24-26 are 'g' (gray background), not a surface. But the token moved UP from [30,24]-[32,26] to [27,24]-[29,26], which means the token is now at rows 27-29, cols 24-26, which is on the gray background.
Wait, that doesn't make sense. The token should only be able to move on surfaces (platforms and bridges), not on the gray background.
Unless... the token can move on the gray background? Or maybe the gray background is actually a surface?
Actually, looking at the segmentation, the background is color 'g' (gray), and the platforms are color 'w' (white/light gray). So the gray background is not a surface.
But the token moved UP from [30,24]-[32,26] to [27,24]-[29,26], which is on the gray background. So either:
1. The token can move on the gray background (it's a surface).
2. The token's movement is not constrained by surfaces.
Let me check the current position again. The ascii shows the token at rows 30-32, cols 30-32. But I moved RIGHT from [21,27]-[23,29] (or wherever the token was), and the token should be at a different position.
I'm getting confused by the coordinate discrepancies. Let me just look at the current ascii and figure out the next move.
Current state:
- Token at rows 30-32, cols 30-32 (on the bridge, next to the wall)
- Wall at cols 33-38, rows 21-41
- Right container at rows 26-30, cols 44-48
The token can't move RIGHT (wall blocks). It can move LEFT back onto the bridge. From there, maybe I can move UP to a higher row where the wall doesn't block.
Actually, wait. Looking at the ascii, the wall is at rows 21-41, which is the full height of the platforms. So the wall blocks at all rows. There's no gap in the wall.
Hmm, but what if the token can move RIGHT from a row above the wall? The wall starts at row 21. If the token is at row 18 (above the wall), it can move RIGHT past the wall's column range.
But the platform ends at row 21. If the token moves UP from row 21, it goes to row 18, which is outside the platform. Can the token move on the gray background?
Actually, I think I need to reconsider the game mechanics. Maybe the token can move anywhere, not just on surfaces. The surfaces (platforms and bridges) might just be visual decorations, and the token can move freely.
But that doesn't match my earlier observations. When I tried UP from [30,30]-[32,32], the token didn't move (only the HUD changed). So the token's movement IS constrained.
OK let me think about this differently. The token is at rows 30-32, cols 30-32. The bridge is at rows 30-32, cols 20-29. The platform is at rows 21-32, cols 20-23.
If the token moves LEFT, it goes to cols 27-29 (on the bridge). From there, if it moves LEFT again, it goes to cols 24-26 (on the bridge). From there, if it moves LEFT again, it goes to cols 21-23 (on the platform).
From the platform at cols 21-23, the token can move UP to rows 27-29, then 24-26, then 21-23. At rows 21-23, the token is at the top of the platform.
Now, if the token is at rows 21-23, cols 21-23, and it moves RIGHT, it goes to cols 24-26. But cols 24-26 at rows 21-23 are 'g' (gray background). Can the token move there?
Actually, I think the answer is NO. The token can only move on surfaces (platforms and bridges). The gray background is not a surface.
So the token is stuck on the left platform and bridge. It can't reach the right platform because of the wall.
Wait, but maybe I'm wrong about the goal. Maybe the goal is NOT to put the token in the right container. Maybe the goal is to put the token in the LEFT container, which is already occupied by the other token.
Or maybe the goal is to move the token to a specific position, not necessarily into a container.
Actually, let me re-read the game description. The goal is to "place tokens into containers." There are two containers: left and right. The left container is occupied by the left token. The right container is empty.
So the goal is to put the right token into the right container. But the wall blocks the path.
Unless... there's a way to bypass the wall. Let me think about this.
Option 1: The wall can be removed or moved. But I don't see any mechanism for that.
Option 2: The token can jump over the wall. But the token moves in 3-cell steps, and the wall is 6 cells wide. So the token can't jump over.
Option 3: The token can go around the wall from above or below. But the wall spans the full height of the platforms (rows 21-41), and there's no surface above or below the platforms.
Option 4: The token can pass through the wall. But earlier attempts to move RIGHT into the wall failed.
Hmm, I'm stuck. Let me try a different approach. Maybe I need to use MOUSE to interact with something.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the game. Maybe the goal is to put BOTH tokens in the SAME container. Or maybe the goal is to swap the tokens.
Or maybe... the right container is not the target. Maybe the target is the left container, and I need to put the right token there too (stacking tokens).
Let me try moving the token LEFT back to the left platform, then DOWN to the left container.
Actually, the left container is at rows 32-36, cols 11-15. The left token is inside at rows 33-35, cols 12-14. If I move the right token to the left container, it would overlap with the left token.
Hmm, let me just try moving the token LEFT and see what happens.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Token at rows 30-32, cols 30-32, blocked by purple wall at cols 33-38. The wall spans full platform height (rows 21-41). Cannot reach right container from current position. Need to reconsider the goal or find an alternate path.
Plan: Move token LEFT back to platform, then try DOWN toward the left container area to test if stacking or swapping is possible.

action(['LEFT', 'LEFT', 'LEFT', 'DOWN'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
